Patch Set 9:

The location of the dynamic library isn't known, since it isn't installed. So libtool uses a mechanism to create a new binary with the hardcoded location of the library the first time the executable is ran.

For instance lustre/utils/lctl is a script, that will use lustre/utils/.libs/lctl to create lustre/utils/.libs/lt-lctl. Every time lctl is run, it will exec .libs/lt-lctl. The problem here is that it's failing to create the lt-lctl binary.

This all goes away when the software is installed, and /usr/bin/lctl is the real binary.

I don't know how your dev setup is. A possible workaround is to run each binary individually after they are built on the build machine (like lctl --version), which will trigger the creation of the lt-* binaries. That will only work if the paths are the same on the build and test machines.

Patch Set 25:

There's a very real usecase for static tools.
The usecase is: when I need to decode a lustre log on an otherwise not lustre-enabled box - I just copy lctl binary there and it works. This patch breaks that.

Can we have a configure option or something to force static linkage of lustre tools with liblustreapi and other lustre libraries please?

Patch Set 25:

> There's a very real usecase for static tools.
 > The usecase is: when I need to decode a lustre log on an otherwise
 > not lustre-enabled box - I just copy lctl binary there and it
 > works. This patch breaks that.
 > 
 > Can we have a configure option or something to force static linkage
 > of lustre tools with liblustreapi and other lustre libraries
 > please?

You can configure lustre with --disable-shared. The external dependencies are then reduced to a minimum. It would also be possible to specifically compile lctl against the static library, or to generate another lctl statically linked.

commit.subject: LU-5541 build: build static and dynamic liblustreapi
commit.message:
LU-5541 build: build static and dynamic liblustreapi

libtool knows how to build both, so no need to hack the Makefile. As
two added benefits, the utilities will now use the dynamic version,
thus reducing their footprint, and calling make twice in a row won't
rebuild objects already built.

Test-Parameters: trivial
